attributes.stem:
A 21-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-year history of progressive weakness in his lower limbs. He reports increasing difficulty walking and has developed stiffness and muscle spasticity in his upper limbs. Over the past six months, he has experienced difficulty speaking and swallowing, with eight kilograms of unintentional weight loss. He denies any sensory disturbances such as numbness or tingling, and has no pain. His bladder and bowel control remain intact. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. There is no history of recent infections or travel.

On examination, he appears cachectic with vital signs within normal limits. Neurological examination reveals asymmetrical muscle weakness, more pronounced on the left side. Fasciculations are visible in both quadriceps muscles. Deep tendon reflexes are symmetrically brisk throughout. He has dysarthria with visible tongue fasciculations. There is no sensory deficit. His legs show no swelling or skin changes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with progressive motor weakness, fasciculations, hyperreflexia, and bulbar symptoms without sensory involvement, which is characteristic of motor neurone disease. The combination of upper motor neurone signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia) and lower motor neurone signs (fasciculations, muscle weakness) with preserved sensation and sphincter function strongly suggests amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), the most common form of motor neurone disease.

Motor neurone disease affects the motor neurones in the brain and spinal cord, causing progressive weakness without sensory symptoms. ALS specifically involves both upper and lower motor neurones, explaining the mixed clinical picture. The asymmetrical weakness, fasciculations (particularly tongue fasciculations), hyperreflexia, and bulbar symptoms (dysarthria and dysphagia) are classic features. The absence of sensory symptoms and preserved sphincter function help distinguish this from other spinal cord pathologies.

Muscular dystrophy primarily affects muscle tissue rather than motor neurones. While it causes progressive weakness, it typically presents with symmetrical proximal muscle weakness and would not cause hyperreflexia or fasciculations, which are neurogenic signs. Muscular dystrophies also tend to have specific patterns of muscle involvement and often show elevated creatine kinase levels. The presence of upper motor neurone signs in this patient makes muscular dystrophy unlikely.

Syringomyelia involves a fluid-filled cyst within the spinal cord that characteristically causes dissociated sensory loss (loss of pain and temperature sensation with preserved touch and proprioception) in a cape-like distribution. While it can cause motor weakness as it progresses, the hallmark is sensory involvement, which this patient lacks. Additionally, syringomyelia would not typically cause tongue fasciculations or bulbar symptoms.

Transverse myelitis presents acutely or subacutely with motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction below a spinal cord level. It characteristically involves sensory symptoms and bladder/bowel dysfunction, both of which are absent in this patient. The gradual progression over years and the presence of bulbar symptoms also argue against transverse myelitis.

Multiple sclerosis is a demyelinating disease that typically follows a relapsing-remitting course, at least initially. While it can cause various neurological symptoms including weakness and spasticity, it almost always involves sensory symptoms at some point. The absence of sensory involvement, the progressive course without remissions, and the presence of fasciculations (which indicate lower motor neurone involvement) make MS unlikely.
learningPoints: Motor neurone disease causes progressive weakness with both upper and lower motor neurone signs without sensory symptoms or sphincter dysfunction.
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis presents with asymmetrical weakness, fasciculations, hyperreflexia, and bulbar symptoms including dysarthria and dysphagia.
references: Foster LA, Salajegheh MK. Motor Neuron Disease: Pathophysiology, Diagnosis, and Management. Am J Med. 2019 Jan;132(1):32-37.
